title: I look at my balance from steady ground
subtitle: A short affirmation for opening the banking app without the spike.
excerpt: A short affirmation for the moment before opening the banking app or checking a balance, the spike of dread, the held breath, the bracing for bad news. Use it in the three seconds before the number loads. The goal is a neutral look, not a braced one.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Stop obsessing over debt." The affirmation shape (present-tense, positive construction, repetition) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement of the late 19th century and modern-form popularisation through writers like Louise Hay (You Can Heal Your Life, 1984).

content.text: A short affirmation for the moment before opening the banking app or checking a balance, the spike of dread, the held breath, the bracing for bad news. The number is the number whatever you feel when you look at it. This affirmation asks for neutral rather than braced: a steady look, not a held breath.

Checking a balance while braced makes the number land harder than it needs to. The spike of fear colours the information before it's read. Steady ground before the look means the number is just a number, useful data, not a verdict. The affirmation creates three seconds of ground before the screen loads.

attrs.quote: I look at my balance from steady ground.

content.text: Once, slow, in the second before you open the app or click through to the account. One breath. Then look. The goal is not to feel good about the number. It is to arrive at it from neutral rather than from braced.

If the debt balance is the specific dread, say it before that screen loads rather than the main dashboard. Repeat this affirmation each time you check rather than working through different ones, the consistency of the same words before the same moment is how it wears in.
If checking the balance has been avoided for days because of the spike, the avoidance needs addressing first.
Open the banking app, exhale, and close it
is a graduated step: look once, breathe, close it before the spiral can start. For the debt obsession loop that runs all day without opening the app at all,

This practice is adapted from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program by Rebecca J Page. That day's work is about stopping debt obsession and finding neutral ground with money. The affirmation shape (present-tense, repetition, positive wording) follows public-domain affirmation craft, with roots in the New Thought movement.
